title: Te Kōrero o Te Awa: The Living Stories of Our River | Ngā Kōrero Ora o Te Awa
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: Students will explore the oral histories and cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream) through the lens of Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau. This stream flows through our rohe and holds stories of tūpuna, traditional fishing practices (hī ika), and environmental guardianship that connect directly to our college's identity and location.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional place names (ingoa wāhi) and their significance along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using appropriate te reo Māori vocabulary
Construct and deliver a short personal narrative (kōrero) in te reo Māori that connects their own experience to the awa using correct sentence structures and past tense
Collaborate in pairs to create a digital story map that layers Māori place names, traditional stories, and environmental observations of the awa
Explain the concept of kaitiakitanga in relation to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and propose one practical action for its protection

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Kōrero: Walking with Stories
activities.description: Guided hīkoi along designated safe sections of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with a local kaumātua. Students will listen to pūrākau (traditional stories) associated with specific landmarks, learn the correct pronunciation and meaning of ingoa wāhi (place names like 'Te Puna a Rongomai'), and record key vocabulary and phrases.

activities.title: He Kete Kōrero: Building Our Story Basket
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students work in small groups to create a shared digital 'kete' (basket) using a platform like Padlet or Google Jamboard. They will collate the new vocabulary, key phrases from the stories, and images from the hīkoi, categorising them into themes such as 'Ngā Ingoa Wāhi' (Place Names), 'Ngā Mahi a Ngā Tūpuna' (Ancestral Activities), and 'Kaitiakitanga'.

activities.title: Taku Kōrero, Taku Awa: My Story, My River
activities.description: Using the collected kete, students will draft, peer-review, and finalise a short personal narrative (5-7 sentences) in te reo Māori. The kōrero will connect a personal memory or experience at the awa to the traditional stories heard, using past tense structures (e.g., I haere ahau..., I kite ahau...).

activities.title: Te Haerenga o Te Awa: Mapping the Journey
activities.description: Using digital mapping tools (e.g., Google My Maps or ArcGIS StoryMaps), student pairs will create an interactive map of the awa. They will layer the traditional place names, embed audio recordings of their own kōrero or the kaumātua's stories, and add current photos with te reo Māori captions describing the health of the awa.

resources: Kuia Ringahora Hemi (Local Ngāti Kahungunu Kaumātua and knowledge holder)
Te Aka Māori-English, English-Māori Dictionary online (https://maoridictionary.co.nz/)
Tablets or smartphones with cameras and audio recording capability for digital story mapping

tasks: Submission of the completed digital story map
Oral presentation of the personal 'Taku Kōrero, Taku Awa' narrative to a small group

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Level 4 - Communicate about past activities and events; understand and use familiar past tense sentences.
Social Sciences: Level 4 - Understand how people pass on and sustain culture and heritage for different reasons and that this has consequences for people.
English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Integrate sources of information, processes, and strategies to identify form and express ideas.
